question_history.text: Why does opening a bank account feel like a punishment?
How does financial anxiety become a product?

annotations.label: Then
annotations.text: Raiffeisen Bank — corporate KYC onboarding. Heavy compliance that frustrated legit users and made them find workarounds. The system backfires.

annotations.label: Reflection
annotations.text: Taxfix. Covid-19. Italian tax system — one of the most complex in the world. Users weren't confused by technology. They were afraid of making mistakes. Design had to hold that fear, not dismiss it.

annotations.label: Now
annotations.text: Now at Pleo — company cards, spending limits, clean books. The fear is still there, just distributed differently between employee and company. I keep wondering whether you can design trust, or whether the product just creates the conditions for it to emerge.

craft_callout: Across Raiffeisen, Taxfix, and Pleo — different countries, different products, same discovery: financial fear is a design problem. You can't solve it by simplifying the interface alone. You have to understand what the person is actually afraid of.

question_history.text: How do you keep a driver's attention on the road, not the screen?
How does a map work when the city changes faster than it can be mapped?
How do we navigate when getting lost is no longer possible?

annotations.label: Origin
annotations.text: HERE Maps — automotive interfaces. How do you give a driver the right information at the right moment without pulling their attention off the road? The map had to disappear. The interface had to work at the edge of conscious attention.

annotations.label: Shift
annotations.text: The Himalayas. No signal, the map is in the sherpa's mind, decisions that mattered. I realised navigation at that scale is less about knowing where you are and more about deciding to keep moving toward something uncertain. The path, not the destination.

annotations.label: Shift
annotations.text: Grab, Southeast Asia. The map became the entire product — real-time, hyperlocal, always updating. Flooding, night markets, street closures appearing and disappearing within hours. The more connected the map became, the less room there was for uncertainty.

annotations.label: Still open
annotations.text: I cannot get lost anymore even if I try. I'm not sure when that happened. I keep wondering what we've given up — not just as designers, but as people who used to need disorientation to find something unexpected.

question_history.text: How does sound shape what we feel before we think?
Can sound carry information more honestly than a screen?
What does sound do that vision can't?

annotations.label: Origin
annotations.text: Conservatory of Music — my thesis research on foley sounds. Metaphorical sounds consistently outperform real recorded sounds in perception tests. A punch sounds more like a punch when it isn't one. That discovery reframed everything: we don't perceive reality, we perceive what our brain has learned to expect.

annotations.label: Shift
annotations.text: HERE Maps — spatial audio for hazard alerts in autonomous driving. A patent filed. Sound as a communication channel — precise, directional, efficient. The most direct path between information and attention.

annotations.label: Still open
annotations.text: Amatori. Ambient sets, sense of community, dubplates cut by hand in Berlin. I keep returning to something more primitive, slow. My DJ sets as a way to reconnect, from the inside.
